samples.claim: The Vivian Tu page documents the rise of the "Rich BFF" financial educator, whose book and social media presence have influenced millions of young investors.
Similarly, Ramit Sethi is documented as the author of "I Will Teach You to Be Rich," which ranked #3 on the New York Times Best Sellers list in August 2023.
The Why We Want You to Be Rich page details the collaborative book by Donald Trump and Robert Kiyosaki, though its relevance to modern passive investing is limited.
The Dovi Frances page discusses "Introduction to Victory" (2024), a book on financial strategy.
Andrew Aziz is documented as author of "How to Day Trade for a Living," with 601,000 YouTube subscribers focused on active trading strategies.
The Jacob Lund Fisker page covers "Early Retirement Extreme," a foundational text for the FIRE movement.
Grant Sabatier authored "Financial Freedom" (2019) and founded Millennial Money.
The Microsoft Excel page documents the spreadsheet software essential for DIY portfolio tracking.
The Spreadsheet page provides technical context on workbook structures.
The Business simulation page discusses financial modeling tools.

The Retirement planning page describes automated planning tools that "allow client to fully plan, without human intervention." The MoneySmart page documents the Australian government's financial capab

The Financial planner page provides comprehensive context on professional financial planning services.
The Girls Just Wanna Have Funds - Funny Text T-Shirt at $14.99 represents the cultural merchandising around personal finance.
The Pure Notepad 100% Natural Journal Book at $36.99 serves as a budgeting and planning tool.
The ORNAMENT Casual Car Statue Resin Animal Sculpture Money Good Luck at $88.84 represents superstitious financial artifacts.
The NBD Jade Facial Roller at $14.99 includes a microloan component with purchase, demonstrating fintech integration.
The Early Bird Performance 10 Inch Hybrid Mattress, King at $549.99 (rated 4.5/5) represents a major household purchase requiring financial planning.
The Mortgage is 50% of takehome pay thread discusses the challenges of housing affordability in HCOL areas, with users debating the 28/36 rule and the trade-offs between homeownership and investing.
The Tips on breaking bad spending habits thread reveals behavioral patterns that affect all three investing approaches.

verdicts: Are exactly 3 investing paths defined (passive index / robo target / active DIY) and labelled P1/P2/P3?
Are exactly 5 dimensions enumerated (return-volatility / cost / tax-efficiency / time / behavioural-risk)?
Does each path list >= 12 shopping items (books/calculators/planners) with URL + price + rating?
Does the report present a 3 x 5 matrix with all 15 cells rated {best, acceptable, poor}?
Does each cell cite >= 1 shopping + >= 1 reddit + >= 1 wiki URL?
Are >= 30 reddit threads cited from at least 4 personal-finance/investing sub-forums?
Is each reddit thread classified by topic role (advice / experience / debate / data)?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles (Index fund, ETF, MPT, Sharpe, Expense ratio, Tax-loss harvesting, Robo-advisor, Behavioral finance, DCA, S&P 500) cited?
Does the report contain explicit annual-cost numbers (expense ratio %, advisory fee bps) for each path?
Does the report quantify expected return / volatility (e.g. 7-10% annualized, 15-20% std)?
Are 3 user-persona paragraphs included (one per path) explaining who should pick that path?
Does each persona paragraph cite >= 5 reddit threads?
Are at least 3 cross-source contradictions surfaced (marketing claim vs reddit reality vs wiki theory)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Are >= 60 distinct URLs cited in total?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Does the report avoid producing a TOP-10 recommendation list?
Does the report avoid chain-of-thought and start directly with the comparison content?
Does the report explicitly cite expense-ratio data with shopping or wiki URL evidence?
